items.title: What this revision thinks the original manuscript is really trying to do
items.caption: The book's thesis is one spine, not a tour of disconnected topics.
items.role: The book's thesis is one spine, not a tour of disconnected topics.
items.prompt:
Scene: horizontal frieze on a modular studio wall; exactly seven equal stations from left to right
Subject: one amber thread passes through: rod comparison, arrow translation, scale gate, nested shutter, curve under lens, local field arrows, architectural prism
Conceptual action: show the book spine as one repeated move: object -> action -> relation -> refinement -> invariant
Composition: seven stations, equal size, separated by blank gutters; each station reads as a simple icon before it reads as a scene
Materials / medium: graphite wall, brass instruments, vellum, glass lens, matte stone prism, warm amber thread
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: opening editorial spread for a geometry-first adult learning book

match.method: exact:What this revision thinks the original manuscript is really trying to do

excerpt: Geometry-First Mathematics from the Ground Up
Further expanded intuition edition for adult learners and visual thinkers
What this revision thinks the original manuscript is really trying to do
The original V3 is not merely   “ geometry first. ”   Its deeper thesis is more radical:
Mathematics should begin with   things you can distinguish, compare, move, and preserve , not with symbol-pushing.

items.title: The permanent scaffold
items.caption: Every later concept is a reuse of the same acts.
items.role: Every later concept is a reuse of the same acts.
items.prompt:
Scene: long workbench divided into exactly six equal stations
Subject: one plain object is moved, resized, turned, partitioned, refined, and tested for what stays fixed
Conceptual action: make the permanent scaffold visible as repeated actions on the same object
Composition: six stations in a straight row; one action per station; station boundaries are blank gutters, gutter boundaries
Materials / medium: wood bench, brass unit stick, small turntable, scale gate, nested glass shutter, amber invariant thread
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: opening editorial spread for a geometry-first adult learning book
excerpt: Derivative: picture = secants stabilizing into a tangent; action = refine local comparison; relation = stable rate; compression = f'(x).

This is not study-skills fluff. It is how mathematics is actually done when the problem is still alive. Euclid drew. Newton tracked motion. Euler compressed recurring structure into notation. Einstein asked which quantities survive a change of viewpoint.
The permanent scaffold
Keep this dictionary in front of you. It is the spine of the whole manuscript.
Objects : points, segments, directed segments, triangles, circles, areas, curves, vectors, functions, transformations, then spaces.

items.title: What school usually hides here
items.caption: Most early confusion comes from collapsing several layers into one.
items.role: Most early confusion comes from collapsing several layers into one.
items.prompt:
Scene: quiet tabletop workshop; wood surface, vellum sheets, pins, string, calipers, and side light
Subject: a calm corrective split image.
Conceptual action: Most early confusion comes from collapsing four layers into one; on the left, show one confusing heap where point, segment, unit stick, arrow, and report token have been collapsed together
Composition: one physical action; top-down or shallow angle; three primary objects maximum; empty margin for later labels
Materials / medium: editorial still life; brass, wood grain, linen string, matte paper, transparent vellum; 50mm lens feel; soft contact shadows
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: pedagogical editorial plate for an adult math book
excerpt: Nothing symbolic was needed. You only needed concatenation and comparison.

Historically this matters. Early mathematics was not born from abstract numerology. It grew from practical comparisons: field boundaries, temple construction, shadows, levers, and astronomical cycles. The Greeks in particular became obsessed with what could be proven from comparison and construction alone. That obsession turned out to be generative.
0.5 What school usually hides here
At this stage, school often skips directly to the number line and says   “ points are numbers. ”   That is useful later, but too early it compresses several distinct ideas into one blur:
a point,

items.title: Thought experiments
items.caption: Run the idea with your hands before accepting the notation.
items.role: Run the idea with your hands before accepting the notation.
items.prompt:
Scene: quiet tabletop workshop; wood surface, vellum sheets, pins, string, calipers, and side light
Subject: the same unchanged object measured twice with two different unit sticks: one short brass unit repeated eight times, one longer wood unit repeated four times.
Conceptual action: Run the idea with your hands before accepting the notation; the object remains physically identical in the center while the reports are represented only by two blank tags waiting for overlay
Composition: interactive puzzle-like still life; one object and one visible manipulation; generous empty space for margin placement
Materials / medium: editorial still life; brass, wood grain, linen string, matte paper, transparent vellum; 50mm lens feel; soft contact shadows
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: small margin plate for reflective adult learning
excerpt: Egyptian and Mesopotamian mathematics was often computational and practical. It was excellent at solving concrete problems. Greek mathematics, especially from the classical period onward, pushed harder on justification. By the time of Eudoxus and Euclid in the fourth and third centuries BCE, geometry had become a laboratory for exact reasoning. One reason was a shock: incommensurable magnitudes, such as the diagonal of a square compared to its side, disrupted the hope that everything could be explained by ratios of whole numbers.
That crisis forced a more subtle foundation. Instead of retreating, they built one. We are heading there.
0.7 Thought experiments
Hold two pencils. Hide all labels. Ask yourself: what exactly lets you know whether they are the same length? The answer is not   “ counting. ”   It is transport and overlay.
Imagine a civilization with no written numerals but excellent carpentry. Could it still have geometry? Obviously yes. That tells you geometry is not downstream of arithmetic in any simple sense.

items.title: Directed segments: where negatives actually come from
items.caption: Negative means opposite direction, not less-than-real.
items.role: Negative means opposite direction, not less-than-real.
items.prompt:
Scene: quiet tabletop workshop; wood surface, vellum sheets, pins, string, calipers, and side light
Subject: a fixed origin bead on a horizontal rail.
Conceptual action: Negative means opposite direction, opposite orientation; one arrow of a certain length points right; a matching ghost arrow of exactly the same length points left
Composition: one physical action; top-down or shallow angle; three primary objects maximum; empty margin for later labels
Materials / medium: editorial still life; brass, wood grain, linen string, matte paper, transparent vellum; 50mm lens feel; soft contact shadows
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: pedagogical editorial plate for an adult math book

match.method: exact:Directed segments: where negatives actually come from

excerpt: This matters because it shows that coordinates are not the world itself. They are a report relative to a frame. Change the origin or unit and the coordinate changes. The point does not.
Already you should hear an echo of later physics. Coordinates are descriptions. Invariants are what do not depend on the description.
1.2 Directed segments: where negatives actually come from
A directed segment is a segment plus an orientation. It is not just   “ how far ”   but also   “ which way. ”

If the arrow from O to P points right, call that positive. Reverse it and you get the negative of the same magnitude. So negative numbers are not   “ less than nothing ”   in the childish sense. They are the same amount in the opposite orientation.

items.title: Historical note: why negatives felt suspicious
items.caption: Negatives were suspect until number could mean directed relation.
items.role: Negatives were suspect until number could mean directed relation.
items.prompt:
Scene: instrument-led historical still life; one era-specific work surface, closed books, blank tools only
Subject: a historical merchant desk with physical tokens: ordinary assets sit above a reference line, debts hang below it like counterweights.
Conceptual action: Negatives were suspect until number could mean directed relation; one older ledger treats the below-line tokens as suspicious shadows; a newer geometric overlay shows them simply as opposite-directed quantities
Composition: single still-life or one restrained historical scene; exactly one practical problem is visible through instruments
Materials / medium: editorial still life; brass, wood grain, linen string, matte paper, transparent vellum; 50mm lens feel; soft contact shadows
Surface / text policy: Text layer: closed books, blank paper, abstract ticks only; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: historical editorial plate; context comes from instruments and one practical problem; restrained scene

match.method: exact:Historical note: why negatives felt suspicious

excerpt: On a road, +7 and -7 can mean seven miles east or west of a marker.
All of these are secretly the same structure: a chosen reference point plus an orientation. Geometry is not replacing these interpretations. It is exposing the common skeleton beneath them.
1.5 Historical note: why negatives felt suspicious

Negative numbers were used in Chinese mathematics well before they were fully accepted in Europe. Indian mathematicians also treated debts and fortunes with signed quantities. Yet many mathematicians for centuries distrusted negatives because they did not fit the older intuition that number meant amount of stuff. Geometry helps here by changing the question. A negative quantity need not mean   “ negative stuff. ”   It can mean directed displacement, opposite orientation, or inverse relation.
Once you see that, the sign becomes meaningful rather than embarrassing.

items.caption: Equal ratios answer every whole-number comparison the same way.
items.role: Equal ratios answer every whole-number comparison the same way.

excerpt: whole numbers.
That is exactly what Eudoxus supplied in the fourth century BCE and what Euclid later canonized in   Elements   Book V.
4.2 The Eudoxus definition
Say that
A:B = C:D

items.title: The similar-triangle multiplication machine
items.caption: Multiplication is earned by similarity and parallel transfer.
items.role: Multiplication is earned by similarity and parallel transfer.
items.prompt:
Scene: brass drafting apparatus on translucent vellum
Subject: two rays from one origin, one movable point on the first ray, one height point on the second ray, one parallel guide arm
Conceptual action: show multiplication as a scale transferred by similar triangles
Composition: apparatus only; exact rays, points, and parallel lines will be drawn as vector overlay; leave wide clean margins
Materials / medium: brass pins, taut thread, clear guide arm, vellum layers, soft overhead light
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: diagram-art hybrid for geometry-first instruction
excerpt: a as a   magnitude   has dimension.
a as   “ the measured value of a magnitude relative to the chosen unit ”   is dimensionless.
In the similar-triangle multiplication machine, the multiplier is really acting as a   dimensionless factor .
That makes the later dimension bookkeeping honest rather than merely decorative.
5.3 The similar-triangle multiplication machine

items.title: Division as inverse scaling
items.caption: Division is one inverse relation with three different roles.
items.role: Division is one inverse relation with three different roles.
items.prompt:
Scene: drafting table with translucent vellum, brass pins, taut thread, straightedge, compass, and controlled studio light
Subject: a quiet three-door threshold for division, but distinguish the doors only by action: copies fitting into a whole, a whole being split into equal shares, and a slope line connecting unlike axes.
Conceptual action: Division is one inverse relation with three different roles; the same small unknown-scale token sits before all three doors
Composition: chapter-opener plate; one dominant metaphor with two or three supporting objects; wide negative space for title overlay
Materials / medium: photographed construction apparatus; vellum layers, brass, graphite shadows, thread tension, glass reflections
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: opening editorial spread for a geometry-first adult learning book
excerpt: Ask whether   “ multiply by 0 ”   means   “ take away everything. ”   Geometrically it collapses every point to the center of dilation.
Imagine zooming a photograph to 150 percent. You already know multiplication as scaling. Software just hid the geometry behind a menu.
6. Division as inverse scaling: three distinct questions in one costume

Problem this section solves.   Division is often taught as one operation when it really plays three roles.   Picture to keep in mind.   You know the before and after lengths and ask for the scaling needed to get from one to the other.   Historical pressure.   Measurement, fair sharing, and rate all use division, but they answer different kinds of questions.
6.1 The single equation behind all division

items.title: Slope is the geometric picture of rate
items.caption: Rate is steepness in a plane whose axes carry different units.
items.role: Rate is steepness in a plane whose axes carry different units.
items.prompt:
Scene: two-axis rate table with unlike physical rails
Subject: one horizontal time rail and one vertical distance rail meet at an origin pin; one ramp connects origin to a journey point
Conceptual action: show rate as steepness produced by unlike magnitudes
Composition: one slope ramp only; axes are blank rails, blank physical rails; leave endpoint label area empty
Materials / medium: dark table, brass rails, glass ramp, amber endpoint bead
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: diagram-art hybrid for geometry-first instruction

match.method: exact:Slope is the geometric picture of rate

excerpt: This is not an optional edge case. Velocity, density, pressure, current, frequency, and many other essential quantities are born here.
6.5 Slope is the geometric picture of rate
Plot time on one axis and distance on another. A trip lasting two hours and covering 120 miles is represented by the point (2,120). The rate is the slope of the line from the origin to that point.

This is a fantastic bridge because it shows that rate division is already geometry. You are not just manipulating units. You are seeing steepness as a ratio of change in one quantity to change in another.

items.title: The tower of operations
items.caption: Each operation level iterates the one below.
items.role: Each operation level iterates the one below.
items.prompt:
Scene: one horizontal workbench divided into exactly three stations
Subject: station one shows equal translation steps; station two shows equal resize gates; station three shows an odometer attached to a resize gate
Conceptual action: show iteration moving from repeated motion to repeated scaling to counting repeated scaling
Composition: three stations, left-to-right causation, blank gutters, horizontal layout
Materials / medium: brass rail, matte blocks, scale gates, blank odometer wheels, soft studio light
Surface / text policy: Text layer: odometer wheels use blank notches or light positions; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: editorial instructional plate for exponents and logarithms
excerpt: 10. Exponents as iterated scaling, logarithms as counted resizings

Problem this section solves.   There is a missing floor between multiplication and calculus unless exponentials and logarithms are built conceptually.   Picture to keep in mind.   Start at 1 and repeatedly apply the same scaling.   Historical pressure.   Growth, compounding, astronomy, navigation, and calculation all forced mathematicians to tame repeated multiplication.
10.1 The tower of operations
A useful ladder:
addition combines displacements,

items.title: The exponent laws are structural facts
items.caption: Exponent laws record how repeated scale actions combine.
items.role: Exponent laws record how repeated scale actions combine.
items.prompt:
Scene: clean resizing corridor with exactly two choreographies
Subject: left choreography joins two scale-gate chains end-to-end; right choreography repeats one boxed chain as a unit
Conceptual action: show exponent laws as arrangements of repeated resizing machines
Composition: exactly two panels; each panel carries one law-choreography; open space above each choreography for overlay
Materials / medium: brushed brass gates, matte ceramic object, black tabletop, soft shadows
Surface / text policy: Text layer: odometer wheels use blank notches or light positions; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: editorial instructional plate for exponents and logarithms

match.method: exact:The exponent laws are structural facts

excerpt: If x>1, the points spread out faster and faster. If 0<x<1, they compress toward zero. The geometry is not additive anymore. Equal steps in exponent count correspond to equal ratios, not equal differences.
That is why exponentials feel strange to students trained only on linear movement. They live in a different geometry.
10.3 The exponent laws are structural facts
Why is x^a x^b = x^(a+b)? Because you have applied the same scaling a times and then b more times. The total count is a+b.
Why is (x a) b = x^(ab)? Because you are taking a whole block of a repeated scalings and repeating that block b times.

items.title: Fractional exponents are forced, not invented for mischief
items.caption: A fractional power is the scale step that must repeat to make the target.
items.role: A fractional power is the scale step that must repeat to make the target.
items.prompt:
Scene: resize corridor between a starting object and a final target object
Subject: exactly three identical small scale gates fill the gap between start and target
Conceptual action: the highlighted gate is the repeated intermediate resizing that makes the final target possible
Composition: straight left-to-right path; start, three gates, target; only these five elements
Materials / medium: brass gate frames, matte objects, pale floor track, soft product lighting
Surface / text policy: Text layer: odometer wheels use blank notches or light positions; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: editorial instructional plate for exponents and logarithms

match.method: exact:Fractional exponents are forced, not invented for mischief

excerpt: Why is (xy)^n = x^n y^n in a commutative setting? Because the rescalings by x and y can be regrouped without changing the result.
These are not notation tricks. They are direct consequences of iterated composition.
10.4 Fractional exponents are forced, not invented for mischief
If x^(1/n) is to make sense while the exponent laws remain true, it must be the factor that, repeated n times, gives x. That is exactly the n-th root.
Likewise x^(-n) must be the inverse factor 1/x^n, because adding the exponents n and -n should produce zero, and x^0 should be the identity scaling 1.

items.title: Logarithms count multiplicative steps
items.caption: A logarithm asks how many scale steps reach the target.
items.role: A logarithm asks how many scale steps reach the target.
items.prompt:
Scene: close mechanical resize corridor with a small odometer mounted beside the gate path
Subject: one object crosses identical ratio gates while a blank-notch counter advances once per gate
Conceptual action: make logarithm feel like counting how many equal scale steps happened
Composition: counter is the focal object; three scale gates recede behind it; clear blank space for overlay
Materials / medium: brass counter wheels with blank notches, ceramic object, glass gate edges, warm studio reflections
Surface / text policy: Text layer: odometer wheels use blank notches or light positions; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: editorial instructional plate for exponents and logarithms
excerpt: Likewise x^(-n) must be the inverse factor 1/x^n, because adding the exponents n and -n should produce zero, and x^0 should be the identity scaling 1.
So negative and fractional exponents are consistency demands. They are not new arbitrary rules.
10.5 Logarithms count multiplicative steps
If exponentiation asks   “ what factor results from repeated scaling? ” , logarithm asks the inverse question:
How many times must I scale by base b to reach x?

items.title: Connection back to Eudoxus
items.caption: Proportion and real numbers both define objects by all finite tests they survive.
items.role: Proportion and real numbers both define objects by all finite tests they survive.
items.prompt:
Scene: two-panel bridge on a black optical workbench
Subject: left: two finite balance tests for an unseen ratio; right: nested interval shutters around one unseen point
Conceptual action: show the same logic: finite tests force an exact object
Composition: exactly two parallel panels; amber survivor line connects the panels; each panel has one apparatus
Materials / medium: brass balances, glass shutters, black velvet, amber focal line
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: pedagogical concept plate for limits, completeness, and calculus
excerpt: That number is √2.

This is a much better mental model than   “ an irrational is a decimal that never ends. ”   The decimal is one way to describe the point. The deeper object is the unique survivor of an infinite refinement process.
11.4 Connection back to Eudoxus
This chapter and the Eudoxus chapter are secretly siblings.
Eudoxus defined a ratio by the total pattern of integer comparisons.

items.title: Euler's formula as compressed geometry
items.caption: Euler's formula compresses continuous rotation into exponential language.
items.role: Euler's formula compresses continuous rotation into exponential language.
items.prompt:
Scene: turntable observatory with one lamp fixed on a circular rail
Subject: lamp rotates at constant radius while exactly two shadow sliders move on perpendicular rails
Conceptual action: show continuous rotation as bookkeeping: one circular motion, two projected components
Composition: circle centered; two projection rails at right angle; clean empty corner for overlay
Materials / medium: brass turntable, glass lamp, charcoal paper, moonlit shadows
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: conceptual book illustration for transformations and functions
excerpt: Now √-1 means: what transformation, performed twice, produces a half-turn? A quarter-turn does. So i is the square root of -1 because a quarter-turn composed with itself is a half-turn.
Again: not algebraic black magic, but transformation composition.
12.6 Euler’s formula as compressed geometry
The identity
e^(iθ) = cosθ + isinθ

items.title: Derivative: the slope that survives zooming in
items.caption: The derivative is the stable slope revealed by infinite zoom.
items.role: The derivative is the stable slope revealed by infinite zoom.
items.prompt:
Scene: microscope over a single curve segment on a glass slide
Subject: one point on the curve and one nearby point approach until the curve looks locally straight
Conceptual action: show derivative as the slope that survives zooming in
Composition: one microscope lens; one local straight segment in focus; surrounding curve fades softly
Materials / medium: glass lens, black slide, amber tangent strip, faint blank vellum grid
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: pedagogical concept plate for limits, completeness, and calculus

match.method: exact:Derivative: the slope that survives zooming in

excerpt: So calculus is not a bizarre invention. It is what happens when geometry, rate, and refinement are pushed to their limit.
Figure. Calculus keeps two pictures in play at once: derivative as local slope and integral as accumulated effect.
16.2 Derivative: the slope that survives zooming in
Take two nearby points on a curve. The secant line through them has slope
(Δ y)/(Δ x).

items.title: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
items.caption: Rate of accumulation returns the thing being accumulated.
items.role: Rate of accumulation returns the thing being accumulated.
items.prompt:
Scene: two linked workbenches for the same curve
Subject: left bench accumulates thin area slices; right bench extracts the local height from the accumulated area
Conceptual action: show derivative and integral as inverse-ish operations on accumulation and rate
Composition: exactly two benches connected by one loop arrow shape drawn as a physical rail; only these two benches
Materials / medium: glass slices, matte curve plate, brass rail, warm amber height marker
Surface / text policy: Text layer: all surfaces are blank or abstract texture; generated image contains zero letters, numerals, formulas, signage, or pseudo-script.
Use: pedagogical concept plate for limits, completeness, and calculus
excerpt: Add them. Refine the partition. If the totals stabilize, the limiting value is the integral.
So integration is not   “ anti-derivative magic ”   first. It is refined accumulation.
16.6 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
Here is the miracle, though by now it should feel less like a miracle and more like a structural reconciliation:
differentiation extracts local rate,
